milestone_description: Transform RASS from a personal-use RAG tool into an enterprise-capable knowledge management platform. This phase introduces multi-tenant knowledge bases with workspace isolation, a proper RBAC permission model, data retention and compliance policies, and production-grade authentication options (SSO/SAML).

issues.title: [Phase D] Implement multi-tenant workspaces with strict data isolation
issues.body:
## Problem

RAGS enforces user-level data isolation through `userId` metadata filtering on OpenSearch queries. While functional for single-user scenarios, this approach does not support:
- Team/organization-level workspaces where multiple users share access to the same documents
- Complete data isolation at the infrastructure level (per-workspace indices)
- Workspace-level billing, quota, and usage tracking
- Organizational hierarchies (org → workspace → user)

In an enterprise setting, multiple departments may need isolated knowledge bases with fine-grained access control and audit trails. The current single-index, metadata-filtered model cannot safely provide this.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Introduce `Organization` and `Workspace` data models
```prisma
model Organization {
  id         String      @id @default(cuid())
  name       String      @unique
  plan       OrgPlan     @default(FREE)
  createdAt  DateTime    @default(now())
  workspaces Workspace[]
  members    OrgMember[]
}

model Workspace {
  id             String          @id @default(cuid())
  orgId          String
  name           String
  openSearchIndex String         @unique  // ws_<cuid>
  quotaMb        Int             @default(500)
  usedMb         Int             @default(0)
  Org            Organization    @relation(fields: [orgId], references: [id])
  members        WorkspaceMember[]
  documents      Document[]
}

model WorkspaceMember {
  id          String   @id @default(cuid())
  workspaceId String
  userId      String
  role        WsRole   @default(VIEWER)
  Workspace   Workspace @relation(fields: [workspaceId], references: [id])
  User        User      @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
}

enum WsRole { ADMIN EDITOR VIEWER }
enum OrgPlan { FREE TEAM ENTERPRISE }
```

### 2. Provision a dedicated OpenSearch index per workspace
- On `Workspace` creation, call `opensearch.indices.create({ index: 'ws_<id>', body: <standard_mapping> })`
- Store the index name on the `Workspace` model
- All document ingestion and retrieval targets the workspace's index

### 3. Update all data access paths
- The JWT payload includes the user's active `workspaceId`
- All ingestion and retrieval endpoints validate workspace membership before processing
- `mcp-server` middleware: `requireWorkspaceMembership(role)` decorator

### 4. Quota enforcement
- Track `usedMb` on the `Workspace` model; update on ingestion
- Return HTTP 429 when the workspace quota is exceeded
- Workspace admins can view usage via `GET /api/workspaces/:id/usage`

### 5. Workspace management API
- CRUD endpoints for orgs and workspaces
- Member invitation flow (email-based for MVP)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A user can create an organization and one or more workspaces
- [ ] Each workspace has a dedicated OpenSearch index; queries are strictly scoped to that index
- [ ] A document ingested by User A in Workspace X is not accessible to User B in Workspace Y (verified by integration test)
- [ ] Workspace quota is enforced; uploading beyond quota returns HTTP 429
- [ ] `WorkspaceMember` roles control access: VIEWER cannot upload; EDITOR can upload; ADMIN can manage members
- [ ] Deleting a workspace deletes its OpenSearch index and all associated documents

issues.title: [Phase D] Implement role-based access control (RBAC) with fine-grained permissions
[Phase D] Implement data retention policies and secure document purge
issues.body:
## Problem

There is no mechanism for:
- Automatically expiring documents after a configurable retention period
- Completely purging all traces of a document (OpenSearch vectors, Redis parents, Postgres metadata, original file) upon a user or admin request — a GDPR/CCPA requirement
- Workspace-level retention policies (e.g., 1 year for enterprise, 30 days for free tier)

Without data retention and purge capabilities, RASS cannot be deployed in regulated industries (healthcare, finance, legal).

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Add retention policy to workspace model
```prisma
model Workspace {
  // ... existing fields
  retentionDays Int? // null = no expiry
}
```

### 2. Implement the `PurgeService`
Create `mcp-server/src/services/PurgeService.js`:
```js
async purgeDocument(documentId, requestedBy) {
  // 1. Fetch document metadata
  // 2. Delete all child chunks from OpenSearch by documentId filter
  // 3. Delete all Redis parent keys (use stored keyPrefix)
  // 4. Delete the original file from disk/object storage
  // 5. Update Document.status = DELETED, record purgedAt, purgedBy
  // 6. Write audit log entry
}
```

### 3. Schedule a retention sweep job
- A BullMQ repeatable job runs nightly
- Queries documents where `uploadedAt < now() - retentionDays`
- Calls `PurgeService.purgeDocument` for each expired document
- Sends a notification to the workspace admin

### 4. Right-to-erasure endpoint
- `DELETE /api/users/:id/data` — admin-only endpoint that purges all documents, chats, and personal data for a user
- Returns a `purgeSummary` listing all deleted resources

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `PurgeService.purgeDocument` removes all traces of a document: OpenSearch chunks, Redis parents, original file, Postgres metadata
- [ ] The nightly retention sweep runs without manual intervention and purges expired documents
- [ ] Workspaces with `retentionDays: null` are not affected by the sweep
- [ ] `DELETE /api/users/:id/data` purges all user data and returns a verifiable `purgeSummary`
- [ ] All purge operations are recorded in the audit log with `requestedBy` and `completedAt`
- [ ] Integration test: ingest a document, call the purge endpoint, verify all storage systems return 404/nil for that document's ID

issues.title: [Phase D] Add SSO and API key authentication for enterprise deployments
issues.body:
## Problem

The current `mcp-server` authentication supports only username/password with JWT tokens stored in `localStorage`. Enterprise customers typically require:
- Single Sign-On (SSO) via SAML 2.0 or OAuth 2.0 / OIDC (Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace)
- Machine-to-machine authentication via API keys (for integrations and CI pipelines)
- Token refresh without re-login (long-lived sessions)

Without SSO, RASS cannot be integrated into enterprise identity workflows, and every user needs a separate RASS account.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Implement OAuth 2.0 / OIDC login
- Add `passport.js` with `passport-openidconnect` strategy to `mcp-server`
- Add config:
```yaml
OIDC_ISSUER_URL: https://accounts.google.com
OIDC_CLIENT_ID: ${OIDC_CLIENT_ID}
OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET: ${OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET}
OIDC_CALLBACK_URL: http://localhost:8080/auth/oidc/callback
```
- On successful OIDC login, upsert a `User` record using the OIDC `sub` claim as the stable identifier
- Issue a RASS JWT with a 15-minute expiry + a secure HTTP-only refresh token cookie (7-day)

### 2. Implement API key authentication
```prisma
model ApiKey {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  keyHash   String   @unique  // bcrypt hash of the raw key
  name      String           // human label
  userId    String
  lastUsed  DateTime?
  expiresAt DateTime?
  User      User     @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
}
```
- `POST /api/api-keys` — generates a raw key (shown once), stores only the hash
- `authMiddleware` checks for `Authorization: ApiKey <key>` header and validates via hash comparison

### 3. Refresh token flow
- `POST /api/auth/refresh` — validates the refresh token cookie and issues a new JWT
- Rotate refresh tokens on each use

### 4. Frontend auth improvements
- Move JWT from `localStorage` to memory (or a short-lived cookie) to reduce XSS exposure
- Display SSO login buttons on the login page when OIDC is configured

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] OIDC login flow works end-to-end with at least one provider (Google or Okta)
- [ ] API key authentication grants the same access as a JWT for the associated user
- [ ] API keys are stored as hashes only; the raw key is shown exactly once at creation
- [ ] Refresh token rotation is implemented; each refresh token can only be used once
- [ ] JWT is no longer stored in `localStorage` in the frontend (moved to memory or HTTP-only cookie)
- [ ] Integration tests cover the OIDC callback, API key auth, and token refresh flows

issues.title: [Phase D] Implement enterprise-grade audit logging and compliance reporting
issues.body:
## Problem

Enterprise customers in regulated industries (HIPAA, SOC 2, GDPR) require comprehensive, tamper-evident audit logs that record:
- Who accessed what data, when, and from where
- All authentication events (login, logout, failed attempts, token refresh)
- All data mutations (create, update, delete for documents, KBs, users)
- All admin actions (role changes, quota adjustments, retention policy changes)

Currently, RASS has no persistent audit logging beyond application-level console output.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Design the audit log schema
```prisma
model AuditLog {
  id          String   @id @default(cuid())
  timestamp   DateTime @default(now())
  userId      String?
  workspaceId String?
  action      String   // e.g. DOCUMENT_DELETE, LOGIN_SUCCESS, PERMISSION_DENIED
  resourceType String? // Document, Workspace, User, etc.
  resourceId   String?
  ipAddress    String?
  userAgent    String?
  outcome      AuditOutcome
  metadata     Json?
  @@index([userId, timestamp])
  @@index([workspaceId, timestamp])
}

enum AuditOutcome { SUCCESS FAILURE PARTIAL }
```

### 2. Create an `AuditService`
Create `mcp-server/src/services/AuditService.js`:
```js
async log({ userId, workspaceId, action, resourceType, resourceId, outcome, metadata }) {
  // Insert into AuditLog table
  // Also emit to structured log (e.g., pino) for real-time streaming
}
```

### 3. Instrument all sensitive operations
- Auth events: login, logout, token refresh, OIDC callback, API key creation/use
- Data events: document upload start/complete/fail, document delete, KB create/delete
- Admin events: role change, retention policy update, quota change, user data purge

### 4. Compliance reporting endpoint
- `GET /api/admin/audit-logs` — paginated, filterable log viewer for org admins
- Filters: userId, action, dateRange, outcome
- `GET /api/admin/audit-logs/export` — CSV export for compliance submissions

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All authentication events produce an `AuditLog` row with IP address, user agent, and outcome
- [ ] All document mutations produce an `AuditLog` row with resourceId and userId
- [ ] `AuditLog` records are append-only; there is no UPDATE or DELETE on this table (enforced by service layer)
- [ ] `GET /api/admin/audit-logs` returns paginated, filterable logs accessible only to org admins
- [ ] CSV export produces a well-formed file with all required fields
- [ ] Integration tests verify that every instrumented operation produces the expected log row
